retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Practitioners follow the most current recommendations from the American Joint Committee on Cancer and the National Comprehensive Cancer Network for more detailed treatment modalities, indications for neck dissections, chemo, and radiotherapy, etc.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Practitioners follow the most current recommendations from the American Joint Committee on Cancer and the National Comprehensive Cancer Network for more detailed treatment modalities, indications for neck dissections, chemo, and radiotherapy, etc. These guidelines are updated annually.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastic carcinoma treatment is generally via surgical resection with 2 to 3 cm margins. Concurrent chemoradiotherapy is an option after resection for positive margins or perineural invasion. In malignant ameloblastoma, surgical resection with 1 to 2 cm margins is usually the treatment modality of choice, and no chemotherapy or radiotherapy is generally required. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment completion will be by a surgeon trained in head and neck surgery, typically an oral and maxillofacial surgeon, otolaryngologist, plastic surgeon, or oncologic surgeon. Often, case planning can include either immediate or delayed reconstruction. Treatment planning is critical, especially when involving teeth, as extractions of teeth in the lesion are often completed and permitted to heal before access through the neck, thus preventing communication with the oral cavity and associated bacteria.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Practitioners follow the most current recommendations from the American Joint Committee on Cancer and the National Comprehensive Cancer Network for more detailed treatment modalities, indications for neck dissections, chemo, and radiotherapy, etc. These guidelines are updated annually.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Though radiation and chemotherapy are not the mainstem of treatment for malignant ameloblastoma, it is important to involve them in select cases where chemoradiotherapy may be of benefit. The entire team should communicate on patient care plans to ensure the best outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Since malignant ameloblastoma is typically a slow-growing tumor, more active treatment approaches such as chemo or radiotherapy may not be necessary. Close patient follow-up for a minimum of five years is necessary to monitor for recurrence.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consider consultation with a dentist, OMS, and/or ENT surgeon.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Mural and solid ameloblastomas are treated with complete surgical excision with normal bone margins at a minimum of five to fifteen mm, though ten mm margins are most common. Reported recurrence rates are up to 70%, with incomplete resection being the most common reason for the high recurrence rate. Enucleation and curettage, cryotherapy and marsupialization have all been used to treat ameloblastomas; however, these modalities are not curative and are not standard of care now.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of ameloblastoma. Outline the typical presentation of a patient with malignant ameloblastoma. Identify the most common adverse events associated with malignant ameloblastoma. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication amongst the interprofessional team to ensure appropriate selection of candidates for malignant ameloblastoma surgery and to enhance postoperative management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: During the study, patients receive dabrafenib orally (PO) two times daily every 12 hours and oral trametinib 2 mg daily for 6 weeks in the absence of disease progression or unacceptable toxicity. Patients with disease determined to be not amenable to resection will indefinitely continue dabrafenib and trametinib as long as there has been no tumor progression. Following the completion of the study therapy, patients are followed up for at least 4 weeks. It is worth noting that this study addressed ameloblastomas, not a malignant variation.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Treatment / Management -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: There is no consensus on the biological behavior of multicystic ameloblastoma and the best way to treat them. However, most agree that aggressive management is essential to achieve a cure. Many modalities have been described ranging from simple enucleation and curettage with a recurrence rate of around 50% to wider resection. [56] [57] [58] [59] Most surgeons advocate resection of the tumor with a 1 cm bony margin which should be confirmed via intraoperative specimen radiography. In terms of the soft tissue, it is recommended to excise one uninvolved anatomic barrier on the periphery of the tumor. The recurrence rate is around 15%. Non-resectable ameloblastoma can be offered radiotherapy. [60] [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Natural History : The vast majority of ameloblastomas are benign and slow-growing, with locally aggressive behavior, which can lead to significant pathology and require extensive surgical treatment. The abnormal cell growth easily infiltrates local tissue, typically bone. Surgical excision is usually needed to treat this disorder. It has a high propensity for local recurrence even with proper surgical management and requires lifelong follow up for surveillance.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Treatment / Management -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Owing to the well-circumscribed nature of the adenomatoid odontogenic tumor lesion and it is being encapsulated, enucleation and curettage are considered to be curative in the majority of cases. [64] There is no consensus on the ideal treatment of calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) cases. This is due to the small number of reported cases and lack of prolonged follow-up. Most surgeons will treat it like ameloblastoma with 1 cm bony linear margin resection and removing the clinically unaffected soft tissue layer as an anatomical barrier. This is due to the highly infiltrative and destructive capacity of these tumors. [50] Finally, for squamous odontogenic tumors cases, most surgeons will treat them by local excision. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Ameloblastoma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: There is a Phase 2 study at Stanford University titled A Pilot Study of Dabrafenib and Trametinib for Patients With BRAF Mutated Ameloblastoma which studies dabrafenib and trametinib in treating patients with ameloblastoma and a specific mutation in the BRAF gene. The study authors believe Dabrafenib and trametinib may halt the growth of tumor cells by blocking enzymes needed for cell growth. The study is currently the only one looking at a specific treatment for ameloblastomas. It started in July 2017 and is currently recruiting. The study has an estimated completion date of August 2020, and they are expected to enroll 17 participants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: inverse_isa of سرطان الرأس والعنق, highlighting its role as a specific manifestation of head‑and‑neck cancer. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate that malignant bone tumors of the skull and facial region are integrated within broader head and neck oncology, can arise as primary or metastatic lesions, and are represented across multiple linguistic and classification frameworks, which is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is identified as a diagnostic finding associated with the disease سرطانة العظم, indicating that secondary tumor deposits have occurred within bone tissue. The presence of a metastatic lesion in a patient suspected of having سرطانة العظم suggests disease spread beyond the primary site, which has important implications for staging, prognosis, and therapeutic planning. Recognizing metastatic lesions through imaging or pathology therefore aids clinicians in assessing the extent of سرطانة العظم, guiding decisions about systemic therapy, surgical intervention, and overall patient management.
